description: Behavioral questions mapped to leadership principles and common interview themes. Uses STAR framework evaluation with follow-up probes designed to test depth of experience.

questions.text: Tell me about a time you disagreed with a technical decision made by a senior engineer or your manager. What was the decision, what was your position, and what happened?

situation: What was the specific technical decision?
Who made the decision and what was their role?
What was the context: timeline, stakes, team dynamics?
task: What was your specific responsibility in this situation?
Why did you feel the need to push back versus going along?
action: How did you raise your disagreement? In what forum?
Did you come with data or just an opinion?
What did you do when they didn't agree with you initially?
At what point did you commit to the decision, even if you still disagreed?
result: What was the outcome of the decision?
Were you right or wrong? How did you know?
What did you learn about when to push back versus when to commit?
How did this affect your relationship with that person?

depth_probes: You said you disagreed. Walk me through the exact conversation. What words did you use?
If you were wrong, how did you acknowledge it?
Would you handle it differently now? Why?
Have you ever committed to a decision you disagreed with and then it failed? What did you do?

questions.text: Tell me about a time you had a conflict with a teammate that was affecting the work. How did you handle it?

situation: What was the nature of the conflict?
How was it affecting the team's output?
task: What was your role in resolving it?
Did anyone ask you to resolve it, or did you take initiative?
action: What specifically did you do to address the conflict?
Did you have a direct conversation with the person?
Did you involve a manager? Why or why not?
result: How did the working relationship change?

depth_probes: What was YOUR contribution to the conflict? Were you partly responsible?
Walk me through the 1:1 conversation you had with this person.
If you escalated to management, at what point and why?

questions.text: Tell me about a time you made a significant mistake at work. Not a small one. Something that had real impact.

situation: What was the project or context?
What was at stake?
task: What were you responsible for?
Should you have caught this earlier?
action: How did you discover the mistake?
What did you do immediately after discovering it?
Who did you tell and how quickly?
What was your remediation plan?
result: What systemic change resulted from this mistake?
What did you learn about yourself?

depth_probes: How quickly did you own the mistake versus trying to fix it quietly?
If you could go back, would you have flagged it sooner? Why didn't you?
What process or system did you put in place to prevent this class of mistake?
Tell me about a time a mistake you made led to a new standard or process.

questions.text: Tell me about a project that failed. What was your role, why did it fail, and what did you do about it?

situation: What was the project and what was the goal?
What was your role on the project?
task: What were you specifically accountable for?
Were there warning signs you should have acted on earlier?
action: When did you recognize it was failing?
What did you do to try to save it?
How did you communicate the failure to stakeholders?
result: What happened to the team after?
What would you do differently if you started over?

depth_probes: At what point did you know the project was going to fail? Did you speak up then?
What was your personal contribution to the failure?
How did you separate 'we failed' from 'I failed'?

questions.text: Tell me about a time you had to make a decision with incomplete information. How did you approach it?

situation: What was the decision?
What information did you have vs what was missing?
What was the time pressure?
task: Why was it your decision to make?
Could you have waited for more information? Why didn't you?
action: How did you gather what information you could?
What framework did you use to decide?
How did you communicate your decision and the uncertainty?
What was your fallback plan if you were wrong?
result: What would have happened if you'd waited?
How did you evaluate the decision after the fact?

depth_probes: What specifically were you uncertain about, and what was your confidence level?
How do you generally decide when you have enough information to act?
Give me an example where waiting would have been the better choice.

questions.text: Describe a situation where requirements were unclear or changing and you had to deliver anyway. How did you manage it?

situation: What was the project and who were the stakeholders?
What specifically was ambiguous?
task: What did you need to deliver and by when?
Who was depending on your output?
action: How did you clarify requirements?
What did you do when requirements changed mid-stream?
How did you keep stakeholders aligned?
result: What would you do differently to prevent the ambiguity?

depth_probes: How did you decide what to build first when nothing was clear?
Did you push back on any requirement changes? When and how?
How do you balance being flexible with stakeholders versus protecting your team from thrash?

questions.text: Tell me about a time you had to convince another team to change their approach or adopt something from your team. You had no authority over them.

situation: What were the teams and what was the relationship?
Why did their current approach need to change?
task: What were you trying to get them to do?
Why couldn't you just mandate the change?
action: How did you build your case?
What was your persuasion strategy?
How did you handle resistance?
Did you find allies on the other team?

depth_probes: What was the hardest objection you had to overcome?
If they said no, what was your escalation strategy?
How do you influence without authority in general?

questions.text: Tell me about a time you worked on a cross-functional security initiative. How did you get buy-in from non-security teams?

situation: What was the initiative?
Which teams needed to participate?
What was their initial reaction?
task: What was your role in driving this?
What obstacles did you anticipate?
action: How did you frame the initiative for non-security audiences?
Did you make trade-offs to get buy-in?
How did you handle teams that refused to participate?

depth_probes: How do you explain security risk to a product manager who just wants to ship features?
When is it appropriate to frame security as a business enabler versus a risk reducer?
Give me an example where you had to compromise on security to get something shipped. How did you decide?

questions.text: Tell me about a time you had multiple critical tasks competing for your time and you couldn't do all of them. How did you decide what to do?

situation: What were the competing priorities?
What was the time constraint?
Who was depending on each task?
task: What criteria did you use to prioritize?
Who did you consult?
action: What did you prioritize and what did you deprioritize?
How did you communicate the deprioritization to stakeholders?
Did you delegate anything?
result: What was the outcome of your prioritization?
Was anything dropped that shouldn't have been?
What did you learn about prioritization?

depth_probes: What framework do you use for prioritization in general?
Tell me about a time your prioritization was wrong and something important fell through the cracks.
How do you handle a manager who says everything is P0?

questions.text: Tell me about a time you had to uplevel a junior team member who was struggling. What was the situation and what did you do?

situation: What was the person struggling with?
How was it affecting the team?
How long had it been going on?
task: What was your role? Were you their manager or a peer?
What outcome were you aiming for?
action: What specific steps did you take to help them?
Did you adjust your approach when initial efforts didn't work?
How did you balance helping them with your own work?
result: What did you learn about developing people?

depth_probes: What's your philosophy on giving critical feedback?
How do you handle a situation where someone isn't improving despite your efforts?
Tell me about someone you mentored who's now more senior than you expected. What did you do right?
When is it appropriate to manage someone out versus continue investing in their development?

questions.text: Describe a time you simplified a complex system or process. What was the complexity, and how did you decide what to cut?

situation: What was the system or process?
What made it unnecessarily complex?
What was the impact of the complexity?
task: What was your mandate?
What constraints did you have?
action: How did you identify what could be simplified?
What did you remove or change?
How did you get buy-in for the simplification?
What was the hardest thing to cut?
result: Did you lose any important capability by simplifying?

depth_probes: What's your threshold for 'this is too complex'?
How do you balance simplicity with handling edge cases?
Tell me about a time you simplified something too much and had to add complexity back.

scoring_guidance_global: BEHAVIORAL SCORING CALIBRATION: Score based on STAR completeness, story specificity, and ownership. 5/5 = Complete STAR with named project, specific actions THEY took (not team), quantified result, and unprompted reflection on what they'd do differently. 4/5 = Complete STAR with good specifics but quantification could be stronger. 3/5 = Mostly complete STAR but vague on actions or results, or says 'we' throughout. 2/5 = Incomplete STAR, hypothetical answer, or clearly rehearsed generic response. 1/5 = Cannot provide a specific example, gives theoretical answer.
